candidates.stmt:
I work for my community not just because it is personally rewarding, but also because I believe that together we can change a deeply unjust and unequal society. Living in Abbey for the past 12 years has been one of the greatest joys of my life. I want to join our hard-working team of councillors to continue doing what I can for our local community and environment.

I’m well prepared for casework. I volunteer for Citizens Advice, so I’m already familiar with the challenges of benefits, food banks and housing in Cambridge. I’m also already embedded in Abbey community life. On the steering committee of the Riverside Area Residents’ Association, I’m spearheading a biodiversity project at the Cellarer’s Chequer and helping develop a vision for the future of Riverside. Together with a fantastic group of locals, I’m setting up a charity to restore the deconsecrated church of St Andrew the Less and turn it into a space for community, heritage and biodiversity.

Abbey is a special place, with great people, that has been labelled the ‘most deprived ward’ in Cambridge for too long. I believe it takes all of us who have the opportunity to make a change – that is why I’m stepping up.

Some priorities for Abbey:

Housing and Cost of Living - I volunteer for Cambridge Citizens Advice every week.  What comes up time after time are housing and cost of living issues.  Abbey residents struggle with substandard council homes, rogue landlords, overcrowded HMOs, fuel poverty, food insecurity and mounting debt.  As your councillor I will not only work with residents to help solve their individual problems, but also work to create a fairer system.

Listening and Acting - I recently spoke at a council meeting as a resident deeply concerned about the direction Cambridge’s development is taking.  The evasive and irrelevant answer I was given proved that I was not being taken seriously.  I will never let that happen when I am a councillor.  I will listen and I will respond honestly and proacti

About Me:

I’m standing to be your local Labour councillor for Abbey because I believe Abbey deserves practical action on the cost of living crisis.  Housing stress being the primary driver of this.

My Background

I’ve spent my career working at the heart of decision-making—advising senior Labour politicians, working in devolved government, and helping organisations navigate complex policy challenges.

Today, I work in the aerospace sector, supporting growth, skills, and world leading research to move this highly polluting sector forward on its net-zero journey—experience I will bring to delivering for Abbey.

Why I’m Standing

Like many people in Abbey, I see both the potential and the pressures in our area:

The need for more genuinely affordable housing.

Pressure on local services and infrastructure.

Concerns about roads, potholes, and day-to-day maintenance.

Making sure new investment reaches every part of our community.

The Labour run council is a national leader in building homes for those who need them most, I want to be part of a group standing up for Cambridge and building rather than blocking.

Rosy Ansell is the 2026 Liberal Democrat Candidate for Abbey Ward. Rosy has lived in Abbey Ward for several years with her family.

If elected to the city council, Rosy will work alongside the Cheney Payne and the Lib Dem team to fight for a fair deal for our city.
David Smith is your Abbey candidate; he has lived in Cambridge for nearly 40 years and works for Cambridge University Press.

I’m standing as your candidate to give working people in Cambridge a real voice. Abbey faces inequality and rising costs, it needs more council homes and community-led decisions. As a local engineer and trade unionist, I see the struggles of the workers who keep this city running. I will fight for residents, not developers or the super-rich. A vote for me is a vote for practical change, fair housing, and representatives who put working people first.

We have the unique legacy of trade unionist and Communist Party branch secretary Ronald Rolph in our ward. He joined the International Brigades and joined the medical auxillery during the Spanish Civil war. Upon returning to Cambridge, he continued to fight for housing, wages and working class involvement in council decisions. Ronald Rolph Court is named for him and his legacy.

A VOICE FOR WORKING PEOPLE
The Communist Party has never been for sale. We cannot be bought-off or silenced. Britain’s Communist MPs and councillors have always stood with working people.

INVEST IN THE PEOPLE OF ABBEY
Abbey needs jobs, housing and investment. We need a council that plans for the benefit of workers, not profiteers and landlords. Let’s build a people’s Cambridge!

FIXING OUR NEGLECTED CITY
Abbey has seen better days. We need planned growth, real community input, and democratic public control and accountability. We deserve better!

I wish to represent the diverse, dynamic community which comprises Arbury. From 2017-2021 I represented Arbury in Cambridgeshire County Council, and know Arbury well having lived in North Cambridge for some 15 years. I have also been a City Councillor (2021-2023) so have experience in both City and County, which is useful in supporting residents with concerns and issues that fit into the City responsibilities or into those for County.

As a councillor, I worked hard in the interests of residents. Amongst other campaigns and achievements, I worked with (then) County Councillor Claire Richards to save Castle Mound and the Shire Hall Green Space around it, so that we could all enjoy access in perpetuity to this important Cambridge landmark - and we won! I also worked on issues such as parking on verges and green spaces, chaired Milton Road Project Local Liaison Forum (LLF) ensuring that the residents' plan for the roadway, footpaths and verges, retaining the trees and gaining more, was implemented instead of the Greater Cambridge Partnership (GCP)'s plan. In this, I ensured that the residents were fully participating members of the LLF, through their residents associations. I also worked with Cllr Mike Todd-Jones and Lilian Runblad (chair and vice chair, Histon Road Project LLF), and with residents who were fully involved as LLF members. I worked with Labour City and County Councillors for more funding for road and pavement repairs, and keeping street lights on, and worked with residents on getting more lights where the changes to street light left great pools of darkness. I am 'at the ready' to work for Arbury, have already held site visits to secure Akeman Street and Darwin Drive on the list for resurfacing and if elected will work to ensure that it is the interests and voices of residents, and your concerns, that are at the heart of my work.

I studied at Cambridge University, am a Barrister and Human Rights Lawyer admitted to practice in all Australian jurisdiction

Fionna grew up in Victoria Park and now lives in the centre of Arbury with her own young family.

After graduating from the University of York, Fionna spent eighteen months working in financial services. She then moved to Southern Mexico to work on a project strengthening community tourism in deprived rural areas. At the conclusion of the project, Fionna moved to Denver, Colorado to work for President Obama's re-election campaign as a Field Organiser.

Fionna returned to the UK at the end of 2012 and took a position as Campaign Advisor to Julian Huppert, Member of Parliament for Cambridge. In September 2013 she began working in Parliament for Martin Horwood MP.

Since 2015 she has worked as a policy adviser, first in Foreign Affairs and now, since the EU referendum, in Foreign Affairs and Brexit.

She now works as a global public health advocate and also wants to give back to the community she grew up in and where she is raising her children.

Fionna is particularly passionate about the future of Arbury Court - and protecting playgrounds and parks in our area.

Fionna says, “We need real change for Arbury. For too long Labour have been taking North Cambridge for granted. As a local Arbury resident, I will listen to you and ensure our area’s voice is heard on the City Council.”

Robert is firmly against the Congestion Charge and is standing both for City and County Council positions. 

Robert is campaigning -Against the proposed Congestion Charge: He organised an open meeting in March with Cambridge residents to discuss this and is holding an open meeting on 26th April in Arbury to discuss residents' concerns

-For Better Roads: Road maintenance is a top priority for Robert, as he is also standing for County Council. This includes fixing potholes, improving road markings and reversing road closures such as the vital Mill Road bridge. 

-For Better Buses: Robert is determined to secure better buses for Arbury. He will fight for the city 1 route, which many elderly people in Arbury rely on. 

Promoted by Timothy Haire on behalf of Robert Boorman, both of 28 Stanley Court, Cambridge CB5 8LW.

I originally moved to Cambridge to study psychology and quickly grew to love this city and community. Now, as your Green Party candidate for Castle ward, I am campaigning for social and environmental justice.

Cambridge is a city of incredible resources and affluence, but remains one of the most unequal cities in the UK. We need to work harder to ensure that everyone in our community benefits from that success. Bridging the “town and gown” divide by breaking down barriers between the university and local residents will lead to a more united and fairer Cambridge. I am also dedicated to protecting our vital green spaces in Castle and across Cambridge. These areas are the lifeblood of our local community and must be protected as such.

Luke first moved to Cambridge for university after growing up in Northern Ireland.

He returned to the city after a year working in Brussels, and decided to make Castle his home on Windsor Road.

In Northern Ireland, Luke was involved in campaigns to improve young people’s mental health, mitigate the impact of Brexit, and build a shared community.

Luke works in Cambridge University supporting young people from under- privileged backgrounds to access higher education.

Luke also volunteers with a local scout group, enabling young people to  gain skills for life. From working in Brussels on UK-EU affairs, Luke knows the harm Brexit has done, and is a tireless advocate for rejoining the EU.

Luke loves the green spaces of Castle and our vibrant community. You’ll often spot him at ParkRun at Eddington Lake or catching up with friends at the Castle Inn.

As a local resident, Luke is delighted to be standing up for his neighbours in Castle Ward as the Lib Dem candidate in May’s election.

As a Castle resident for 23 years, with children educated at Mayfield and Chesterton, I understand our community and am proud to serve you as your Labour City Councillor. On 7th May, I am seeking your support for re-election to continue that work.
Over the past five years, I have worked full time as your Councillor to improve local services, strengthen community safety and promote tolerance across our diverse neighbourhoods. I am committed to restoring our natural environment, protecting chalk streams, achieving sewage-free rivers, and tackling climate change. I have decades of experience in local government, planning and development. Over the last three years as Cabinet Member for Finance and Resources I have turned a forecast deficit into a balanced budget.

1. I am against reintroducing congestion charges.
2. I will defend and maintain green spaces and children's playgrounds.
3. I will curb antisocial behaviour, e.g. very fast and dangerous driving in Cambridge.

**They want to confuse you by endorsing all kinds of weird causes, so you forget what's really at stake: you.**

As the Greens, LibDems and Labour, the parties that dominate Cambridge, sink more and more into populist stunts and extreme ideology, what do you think about it? What hopes can you have for your future, your family’s future, your security...when it all lies in the hands of people who are trying to impress their fans through irrational, foolish acts bordering on sedition?

How safe are your interests in their hands? While you despair about the potholes, crime and dismal infrastructure in your areas, they are screaming at demonstrations in solidarity with various dodgy overseas organisations and regimes, dancing with strippers, clowning around.

I’ve spoken to a lot of people in our ward who are very worried. The only way to overcome this is: **don’t put your future in their hands.**

You've voted for them for years, but lately they have grown feral. Your needs, your concerns, your future? Not interesting. They have bigger fish to fry. Have you seen their antics? The things they say, sometimes behind closed doors, sometimes not? The people they associate with? Do you still think you can trust them? Perhaps you haven’t seen or heard? Would you care to look into it? You’ll be shocked! Do your research, ask around: it’s all out there. Time for a change, before it’s too late. Democracy is not about picking your favourite team or colour. It’s about ensuring you are represented by people who will fight your corner.

Trust me, I’m a doctor. I’ve been fighting for people’s lives for 25 years.

Vote for me, Dr. Khalid, on 7 May 2026. I’m not for any party. I’m Independent. For Castle, for our community, our families, our future.

My wife Carole & I have lived in Church End Cherry Hinton since 1983. We were married in St Andrew’s Church Cherry Hinton in 1978. 
I was employed as the security student liaison manager at Cambridge Regional College for 15 years before retiring from that role in 2019 and am currently working as their first aid instructor. I hold an instructor licence for Mental Health First Aid and for over 20 years have been a volunteer Community First Responder for East of England Ambulance Trust covering the Cherry Hinton area, and as volunteer trainer for their first responder scheme. I take immense pleasure in passing on life-saving skills whenever can, and some of these sessions 'Fast First Aid' I run over 90 minutes on the first Saturday of the month at our new community hub on the High Street.

I have been involved in the local community for many years in numerous ways - such as with Cherry Hinton Royal British Legion both as poppy appeal collector and resident bagpipe player at remembrance service every year, as a committee member of Cherry Hinton Residents Association, as the health & safety advisor for our community hub, with the Neighbourhood Watch – where I was chair for 10 years, the Friends of Cherry Hinton Hall, and the Friends of St Andrew’s. Of course, I have also had the honour of representing Cherry Hinton as a City Councillor since 2004 – a highlight of which was being Mayor of Cambridge in 2009/10, 2019/20 and in 2020/21.

Over the years I have been involved in many local campaigns and initiatives including securing environmental improvements, helping raise money to secure CCTV on the High Street, and more recently helping with the plans which finally delivered the excellent community hub complete with the Cherry Branch café.

I continue to be passionate about Cherry Hinton, my continued work with many residents on a wide variety of issues all year round (not just at election times!) is proof of my continued commitment to our village and its residents. We have

I’ve called Cherry Hinton home for 11 years, and I know the power of everyday community. In 2022, I organised a Front Door Festival that brought neighbours together for the first time in decades, sharing food, music, laughter and raising funds for the Cherry Hinton Hub.

I’m standing because I believe every street can feel like that: connected, creative and caring. I dream of a Cherry Hinton where there's a Front Door Festival in every street and neighbourhood. Where neighbours know each other and share skills, tools, meals, their time and joy.
Where we hear birdsong and the buzzing of bees instead of speeding motorbikes and the youth address their trauma through art and music instead of resorting to anti-social behaviour. Where the most vulnerable are looked after, making everyone else better off for it.

My strengths lie in organising, facilitating, empathising, envisioning and building community. My background spans human‑centred design in tech and hands‑on work with organisations like Jimmy’s Cambridge, improving services for people experiencing homelessness. I understand how systems, behaviour and trauma intersect, and why kindness and community matter more than ever as we face rising costs, extreme weather and growing instability.

We’re in the same boat, facing the same storms. But when we look after each other, we’re stronger.

Together, let us build a safe, strong, fair and vibrant Cherry Hinton.
Who's with me?

I am Dr. Ahmad Rushdhi, also known as Dr. Ru or DrAR, and I am the Liberal Democrat candidate for Cherry Hinton in Cambridge. Having moved to Cambridge from Sri Lanka in 2022, I have made Cherry Hinton my home. This has provided me with the opportunity to engage closely with local residents and gain a comprehensive understanding of the critical issues affecting our community.

As a physician specializing in clinical nutrition, my professional focus is on preventive medicine and public health. I am affiliated with Addenbrooke’s Hospital and also maintain a practice in London. My work is dedicated to disease prevention and promoting healthier lifestyles through education, nutrition, and early intervention. I firmly believe that enhancing public health extends beyond healthcare services to encompass the development of robust community well-being.

Since my arrival in Cherry Hinton, I have actively participated in community life, regularly interacting with residents from diverse backgrounds, including those employed in the NHS and other essential services.

Residents have communicated several practical concerns, including traffic congestion, road maintenance and potholes, and the enhancement of neighborhood safety. I am committed to collaborating with the council, police, and community partners to address these issues and implement effective solutions.

Additionally, concerns have been raised regarding anti-social behavior in the vicinity of certain Voi e-scooter parking areas, as well as knife crime, disruptive moped noise, and theft around local supermarkets. I will work in conjunction with neighborhood police teams and residents’ associations to support coordinated action and strengthen community safety initiatives.

Public health remains a paramount passion of mine. I collaborate closely with General Practitioners and other healthcare professionals, and I aspire to promote local community health awareness programs. Furthermore, I also share health educatio

Sarah has lived in Cambridge for over 20 years. As a mother who has worked in the housing sector and as a teacher, she has observed the inequality of opportunity that is so striking in Cambridge. Sarah says "An obsession with growth has left Cambridge with threatened water supplies, a stripped-back green belt, a broken transport system, unaffordable house prices and shocking inequality. The Greens are the only local party challenging "business as usual." Our record of listening to, and working with, residents saw the Cambridge Green Party draw level for second place in the May elections. There is now a real opportunity in Cambridge to send a strong environmental message to the next government. Want Green? Vote Green!

Coleridge has been my home for over 20 years, and I have had the privilege of being a teacher to many of our young people in local schools. I have been a local councillor for over 10 years. On 7 May, I am standing for re-election so that I can continue delivering for our communities here in Coleridge.

My priorities for Coleridge:

**Build communities, not just homes.** Tackle anti-social behaviour. Champion community grants, food hubs, holiday lunches. Build a new community room and improve green spaces.

**High quality homes for all.** Continue building a new generation of green council homes, including at Fanshawe Road, so that local people have great places to live. Support residents in need with housing and address rough sleeping.

**Cheaper, easier, safer travel.** Improve Cherry Hinton Road cycleway, tackle speeding cars, scooters and e-bikes, fix potholes, fight to save the Tiger Pass, champion better buses.

I’m already working hard for Coleridge. I’ve challenged poor planning decisions, and stood up for local businesses, including shops on Cherry Hinton Road. I’ve installed outdoor gym equipment. I made sure the number of council homes built on Fanshawe and Davy is more than before (up from 52 to at least 94). I brought in the Tiger Pass for young people and free pre-9.30am travel for concessionary bus pass holders.

Most of these things started as issues raised by fellow local residents like you. We have achieved all these improvements working together. But there’s so much more to do.

I want to continue to work with you to address other local issues and secure improvements that can benefit our local community and make Coleridge an even better place to live.

Tim is the local, hardworking choice for Coleridge.

Tim is a retired parent and IT Consultant.

Tim has lived in Cambridgeshire for 40 years; Coleridge is now his permanent residence.  Tim's favourite pastime is hiking, with my wife, anywhere in the world.

If elected Tim will work with Cllr Karen Young and Parliamentary Candidate Cheney Payne to fight for a fair deal for Coleridge.

Sarah Haithcock lives in East Chesterton and works at a local special educational needs school. She has dedicated her professional life to service and wishes to bring that same commitment to the City Council as your representative.

As your councillor, she will continue to listen to your concerns, deliver practical solutions and make our area safer, greener and fairer.

Having spoken with hundreds of residents, her key priorities for East Chesterton are:

1. Building strong and resilient communities
She will work with the community and local authorities to tackle anti-social behaviour such as drug dealing, speeding, littering and fly tipping to make East Chesterton safe for everyone.

2. High quality homes for all residents
She wants to support the building of a new generation of environmentally friendly and accessible council homes. She will support residents in need of housing and work with community groups, authorities and charities to tackle rough sleeping. She is committed to protecting renters rights and improving access to affordable homes.

3. Cheaper, easier and safer travel
She will campaign for more frequent local buses, protected cycle lanes and safer pedestrian crossings.

Sarah has been active in the community beyond her professional life. She has listened to hundreds of residents over recent years and supported individuals with their specific issues.

Her work in the community includes:

- Leading police walkabouts to identify anti-social behaviour hotspots with local authorities
- Challenging the County Council on the dimming of streetlights across the city
- Campaigning against cuts to holiday food vouchers for the most vulnerable children in our community

Ania has lived in Cambridge since 2015. Having grown up in Poland, Ania is one of many scientists who have come to Cambridge for work and decided to make it their permanent home.

Having worked for a Cambridge-based company for the past 10 years, and now taking on a freelance role, Ania is keen to make sure that Cambridge works for everyone equitably – residents, businesses, visitors and nature.

She is also passionate about evidence-based medicine, and using best available data to make healthcare decisions.

Her love of dance led her to volunteer for a local dance society to increase access to affordable community social classes.

In her spare time, Ania is learning to play the trumpet and trains with a local club in Latin Formation dancing. Ania is also a keen cyclist and loves going on walks with her corgi.

If elected she will work with Bob Illingworth and Ian Manning to stand up for East Chesterton residents and make your voice heard.

I decided to get more involved in politics because I’m angry and I think I share that anger with a lot of people out there.

Our communities are falling apart, with literally crumbling infrastructure and an ever-widening class divide.  It’s outrageous that a city as wealthy as this one does so poorly by its residents.

I’ve lived in eight cities across three countries and I’ve seen great examples of how Cambridge could be cleaner, safer, and greener.  The blueprint is out there, and it’s time somebody started pushing us in the right direction.

David Creek is the local, hardworking choice in this election - who will work with Lib Dem Parliamentary Candidate Cheney Payne to stand up for King’s Hedges.

David, 62, lives in King’s Hedges and went to the local primary school St Laurence’s, Arbury Road when it first opened 1968.

David grew up in East Chesterton before moving to King’s Hedges in 1983. He cares passionately for our area.

He attends Church each Sunday and helps deliver quarterly Church newsletters in this ward. David is a Technical Manager at a food Company in Histon which he first joined in 1981.

In his spare time David enjoys walking, cycling, going to the theatre and spoiling his cat Splodge who is “a local area celebrity.” As well as being a real Ale drinker supporting our local pubs, David volunteers at the beer festival each year.

David says “The main reason I love living in King’s Hedges is how great and friendly people are irrespective of political views, age, nationality, colour, sexual identity and religious beliefs. Let’s not lose what makes our community a special place to live.”

I am a carer, public servant (forensic scientist), trade union member, activist owner of 3 rescue cats. I was born in and grew up in Kings Hedges in a council home. I know the struggles of the working class because I live those struggles, unless you are a millionaire we are all struggling nowadays. Everything is broken and everything is more expensive. Britain is broken but not because of the migrants, it is broken due to decades of unequal wealth distribution and privatisation.
I am standing as an independent because I am fed up of the mainstream party councillors who do not live here, making decisions about us without involving or consulting us, behind closed doors. All they ever care about is showing up around election time for our vote. The other candidates just care about potholes and litter. Indeed, those are issues we need to fix but our issues in Kings Hedges go deeper than potholes and litter. Our issues are due to decades of austerity and deprivation. Kings Hedges is one of the many deprived wards in Cambridge. It is disheartening that we have high food bank usage, in a city tipped to be the next Silicon Valley by the PM. There is nowhere for the youth to play, learn new skills or make new friends. Many cats are run over and accidents happen due to careless driving in Kings Hedges. If elected, want to open a permanent youth club in the ward. I want more CCTV, lighting, and to repair potholes and cracked, uneven paving so that people feel safe getting around Kings Hedges and that cars aren’t subject to costly repairs. I want people to feel safe in and enjoy living in Kings Hedges, we need to thrive not just survive. I will hold regular people’s assemblies with the community so that we can have our say on the council of what we want for our ward. I will campaign for the Cambridge weighting (HCAS) and the real living wage to be increased. I will campaign for free bus passes for carers or disabled individuals and for reliable, green, cheaper public transport f

Dear neighbour,

My name is Eleanor Cooke, and you may have seen me around litter-picking, visiting my grandfather or, in the case of fellow residents on Lovell Road, knocking on doors trying to return a lost dog.

I didn't plan to get into politics. I am running for city councillor in Kings Hedges because, frankly, I am terrified.

Other people my age (I admit I'm young to be doing this) are drifting to extremes, choosing among parties that I find populist and dangerous. I'm scared of where that's going to end, just like I'm scared of any number of worldly issues, like misinformation and a lack of community.

Like most people, I have strong views on many issues. I just don't think they really matter for a local representative. Because this isn't about what I believe, or what you believe. It's about local issues, being dealt with by local people.

If elected, I will be easily accessible. I will go door-to-door working out the biggest problems in Kings Hedges and how to solve them.

Although I am running as an Independent, I am endorsed by two other Independents: David in Newnham and Khalid in Castle. Both are decent, and I believe they will serve the residents in their wards faithfully.

I can't knock on your door right now to speak for myself: due to problems moving house, I am stuck in Kent for the next few weeks. It's unfortunate timing... But you can contact me at REDACTED

You've been living with poor options recently. Maybe you've even given up on the local elections entirely. So I'm asking you to take a chance. If I am elected, it will be worth it.

Thank you for taking the time to read this.

Yours with hope,

Eleanor

Published and promoted by David Carmona, 5 Beagle Road, Cambridge CB3 0UH, for the electoral campaign of Eleanor Cooke

Katie has lived in Market Ward, near the Grafton centre, for nearly 18 years and has been your city councillor since 2019.  She has worked in Higher Education in Student Services for three decades and until very recently was employed at Anglia Ruskin University for more than 17 years as a Student Adviser, dealing with students in distress or with ongoing health conditions to help them succeed in their studies.

She is also a parent to a teenager and was a regular volunteer at the School Streets barrier at St Matthew's school whilst her child was at school there.

She is passionate about local democracy and is proud to have worked with fellow Lib Dem councillors to have successfully reduced single use plastics on our open spaces and stopped herbicide use on our green spaces. She fights on for better basic services in the city centre and around Cambridge - particularly public toilets. She has worked hard to ensure that events taking place on our public open spaces reduce their impact on the grass, arguing for proper funding from event organisers to repair and upgrade the soil and to return spaces to public use as soon as possible.

During her time as your councillor in Market ward, she campaigned successfully to keep the cows on our commons to increase biodiversity, to save trees in the ward from felling, and as our Planning and Transport and previously Housing spokesperson, has worked hard to improve housing around the city, including desperately needed affordable homes and the provision of decent timely infrastructure like schools and GP surgeries.

Katie has been a long standing advocate for the opportunity for part time and flexible working for all who want it, as well as for the wider Equalities agenda to encourage more diversity in the workplace and in politics. She understands that for many issues affecting our wards, local politics is about getting things changed for the better and working collaboratively with whoever can help to achieve this. It is often thes

I moved to Cambridge to begin my studies in biomedical sciences and quickly fell in love with the community, town and history of Cambridge.

With so much turbulence and negative media around it’s now more important than ever that we band together : from the smallest community, to our national identity. I want to ensure we are creating a future where people are the beating heart of our community and city.

I have a broad experience including training fire fighters, as a field medic and in the biomedical world. I am standing to help make an environment where all types of people feel listened to, supported and heard. From making more spaces for minorities to come together and thrive, to bringing groups together in celebrating the diversity of our city.

Hi, I’m standing in Market ward because I think the council has drifted away from everyday concerns.
Too many decisions are being made without thinking through the real-world impact—on housing, on small businesses, and on how people actually get around the city.
I support Reform UK because they’re pushing for practical, common-sense policies—lower costs, better value for taxpayers, and more accountability. Locally, it’s about making sure Cambridge works for the people who live and work here.
That means better scrutiny of developments, supporting local businesses, and making sure policies don’t make everyday life harder or more expensive.
I want to bring a bit more balance and realism back into how decisions are made here in Cambridge.

I have lived in Newnham for 16 years and am deeply involved in the local community. My children attended Newnham Croft Primary School, where I have been a governor for the past four years and currently serve as Vice-Chair. I also volunteer at St Mark’s Church and help coordinate a monthly collection for the Cambridge City Food Bank.

Alongside this, I work in the environmental field, focusing on how scientific evidence can be used to support better real-world decisions. I care strongly about sustainability and fairness, and about making sure that local and global challenges are addressed in ways that are practical and effective.

I have been out speaking with residents across the ward and value the opportunity to listen to people’s experiences and concerns. If selected, I would work closely with Newnham’s existing Green councillors to represent the community with care, integrity and common sense.

I would be proud to serve as a Green councillor for Newnham and to help support a greener, fairer Cambridge.

Katie moved to Cambridge when her son started school. As a mum and teacher in Special Needs, Katie is in touch with the experiences of a wide diversity of Cambridge residents, aware of the opportunities and difficulties of living in this city. In her spare time, Katie enjoys walking, writing and cheering her son in football and hockey matches.

Katie has always been passionate about saving wildlife and this year has taken the no-flight pledge. Within the Liberal Democrats, Katie is a member of the Green Lib Dems and the Social Liberal Forum, both of which explore and promote holistic solutions to the destructive tendencies of our current economy.

Katie believes that the widening wealth gap in the UK underlies many of our problems, blocking transition to greener ways of life, incubating hate speech and placing ever increasing demands on healthcare and social services. Within the Lib Dems and beyond, Katie is researching and campaigning to reduce tax on the lowest paid and cancel tax reliefs for the highest paid.

Cambridge is changing fast. Money is flooding in, poverty and crime are rising, our infrastructure is falling apart. The big political parties have turned local elections into a travesty of ideology and vested interests. Only Independents are free of party political shackles, and able to truly work for their constituents.

I am part of a group of Independents headed by Dr Abu-Tayyem. At least two of us are standing in Cambridge in the 2026 local elections. In addition to being an Oncology consultant, Dr. Abu-Tayyem has had a lot of experience and has extensive personal contacts in Politics and International Relations, at the highest level. He is determined to make a positive impact in a political scene that is rife with extremism and populist shenanigans.

If elected we will provide the residents of our respective wards with genuine representation, ensuring that their interests and concerns are heard and taken into consideration. We will also expand our alliance to cover as many of the wards of the city as possible, with the aim of having a candidate standing in every ward in the 2027 Unitary Authority elections.

We will work with all stakeholders to improve our city and give us all a more secure future, based on one prime consideration:

The people around us are the best guarantee of our security and well-being.

**1** strengthening and empowering our communities, 
**2** encouraging volunteering,
 **3** fixing things ourselves when others won’t, 
**4** improving our public transport, 
**5** making streets safer for cyclists and vulnerable pedestrians, without penalising cars, 
**6** improving access to low-cost housing and **7** dental care, 
**8** ensuring that any mass-development of our city is properly planned and thought out.

I am deeply connected to Cambridge; I was born here, studied here and now work here. Cambridge is one of the most unequal and nature-deprived places in the UK, and those things are intrinsically linked: we will not avoid climate collapse without reducing inequality.

As a councillor, I will prioritise the care of those who are most vulnerable, including our growing elderly population, the disabled community, and people who are homeless or in insecure housing. I will also be a voice for young people in Petersfield who are bearing the brunt of the mental health crisis due to climate anxiety and lack of amenity investment. I hope to put local people and our natural world back at the heart of our communal decision making.

I have long been passionate about equality and community support. During the pandemic, I volunteered for Cambridge Community Kitchen and Waterbeach Foodbank. I am a proud feminist, LGBTQAI+ ally and antiracist, and care greatly about accessibility for people with disabilities. Due to my background in architecture and landscape design I am well-versed in the policies that have led to the housing crisis, and what we can do about it. I’m in favour of more council housing and introducing rent controls so tenants can have greater stability.

Hello, I'm Cameron, the Labour leader of Cambridge City Council, and I would love to have the opportunity to serve as a councillor in Petersfield ward. I’m standing because I care deeply about improving people’s lives and building a fairer, kinder society. Cllr Richard Robertson will be stepping down this year — he has been an incredibly dedicated and effective councillor, and I hope that I can go some way to following in his footsteps.

I've lived and worked in Petersfield, and I want to make sure that, as our city grows, we protect and improve amenities and quality of life for Petersfield residents.
Many residents have raised with me concerns about crime and antisocial behaviour. I have a professional background in prison education, and will use my knowledge of crime prevention to work with police and residents on issues such as drug dealing and shoplifting.

We all want to improve the state of our roads and pavements, and to make it safer to walk and cycle. For Mill Road specifically, I will work with local residents, business owners and fellow councillors to try to find a way forward that can gain broad-based consensus and make Mill Road a safer, more pleasant place to be.

I love the diverse, welcoming nature of Petersfield’s communities. As a modern languages graduate, I’m committed to promoting multiculturalism.
Homelessness is a key issue I am working to tackle in Petersfield and across Cambridge. Over the last year, I have co-chaired and supported the development of the Cambridge Homelessness Charter, a new alliance of councils, charities, businesses and universities working to reduce homelessness as far as possible.

The City Council recently stopped using herbicides, to help butterflies, bees and other pollinators to thrive, but we do need to clear weeds where necessary. The Labour-run City Council has just put an extra £80,000 into clearing weeds in the next two years.
 
As Leader of the City Council, I’m proud that Cambridge is leading the world in medi

Sam first moved to Cambridge for work 6 years ago, and immediately fell in love with Petersfield. He has lived here with his partner ever since.

Sam is a full-time campaigner, with experience in winning change for local people.

Sam lives in the heart of Petersfield and understands the issues - local and national - facing our community, and its businesses, pubs and green spaces.

Sam says, "If elected I will stand up for our city’s values and will work with other Lib Dems to hold Labour to account on issues like Europe and Gaza.

Contact Sam: samnjoliver97@gmail.com

Amanda has lived in Cambridge for over 30 years. She lives on Cherry Hinton Road and works at Cambridge University Press.

Over two decades of service as a councillor has earnt her a reputation for hard work — and for getting things done.

Amanda was an Executive Councillor when the Lib Dems ran the City Council, responsible for services from customer services to council property management. As a County Councillor she chaired the Cambridge Traffic Committee.

Amanda knows local politics is about everyday life. On both the City and County Councils, she consistently stood up for Queen Edith’s residents, making sure local voices are heard.

Contact Amanda - amanda@ajtmail.co.uk

Long-term Cambridge resident
Historian, teacher, researcher, carer

I’ve lived in Cambridge for thirty-five years, working as historian, lecturer and teacher, and editor. Most importantly, I’ve been a parent and a carer.

The climate emergency is upon us.  It needs to be at the centre of every decision and every action by the City Council.

Active transport  - by foot, by cycle and by bus  - is crucial.  It builds community and connection, and doesn’t pollute and cause congestion.  Cambridge is still in thrall to the car.  Having spent years trying to get around with small children, and at times suffering disabling back pain, I know the difficulties many of us face in negotiating city streets.

I am passionate about the quality of our public spaces and about the built environment. Too much of what is being built is too big, too high and too ugly.   We risk turning into a vast megapolis, losing everything which makes Cambridge such a good place to live. Cambridge deserves better.

I was heavily involved with the the PTA of my daughter’s primary and secondary schools, participate in the Happy Bee Streets initiative allowing the wild flowers in our verges to grow, and serve on the premises committee at Quakers, helping manage the building and its upkeep.

I like nothing better than talking and listening to people, finding out about what works and what doesn’t.  If elected to the City Council, I would seek to listen and to serve our local and city-wide community

My name is Maruf Ahmed I am your Labour councillor candidate and
care deeply about our community. Through my work with the

I’m especially proud of helping raise funds for the Cambridge

 My priorities are simple:

I’m Rosy Greenlees and I am standing to be a City Councillor for Romsey because I want to give back to the community and the city I have lived in for 30 years. I have been knocking on doors for several months hearing residents' views and listening to their concerns.

I will be your local champion acting on your issues  big or small. Romsey has a great diversity which is seen in its many communities, shops and places of worship.

My focus will be on:
 
1. Build communities, not just homes
Tackle anti-social behaviour, champion local communities and protect green spaces. 
Residents want clean and sage public spaces. I will work with local community groups and resident associations to ensure we have a tidy and well kept locality and that we improve our green spaces. I will lobby the County Council to improve  terrible state of our roads and improve safety for drivers, cyclists and pedestrians.

2. Cambridge homes for all residents
Continue building a new generation of homes and support residents in need.  Labour is protecting private renters, funding new council staff to support tenants and tackle rogue landlords. I will work with residents and the council staff to protect tenants and ensure that housing in Romsey is fit for purpose.

3. Champion our diverse community in Romsey
Creating an equal, safe and green Romsey where all residents, local businesses and visitors feel welcome and supported.  
Mill Road is the heart of our community, My vision is to make it the best street for independent shops serving our local residents and Councillors on both sides of Mill Road bridge. I will work to secure funding to enhance the streetscape, improve the environment and make the street safe for everyone.

If elected I will 
- hold regular public surgeries for advice and help; 
- regularly walk the ward to report potholes, fly-tipping and other local issues;
- be your voice on the City Council to ensure that your views are listened to when making decisions.

Hello - I am Olaf Hauk, one of your three City Councillors for Trumpington.

I was first elected in 2021, re-elected in 2022, and I am standing again in this May’s City Council election. It has been a real privilege to represent and work for Trumpington residents over the past five years.

During this time, I have focused on practical improvements that make a difference to everyday life — from helping secure CCTV near fly-tipping hotspots, to getting overgrown vegetation cleared along busy Trumpington Road footpath, working with police and council officers to tackle anti-social behaviour, getting the council to allow traders on Hobson Square, and assisting tenants facing issues with their housing associations. I have also worked with my local Liberal Democrat colleagues and MPs to make the case for a fairer deal for Cambridge. It has been rewarding work, but there is still much more to do. I am ready for new challenges.

I have worked in Trumpington for over 25 years as a cognitive neuroscientist and have lived in several parts of Cambridge during that time. I was born in Germany and am now proud to be both a British and German citizen, and a committed European.

Cambridge is a place that attracts people from across the world to learn, work, and build their futures. That brings huge opportunities, but also real challenges — particularly around affordable housing, transport, and the environmental impact of growth. Trumpington, as the city’s largest ward and home to major new developments, sits right at the heart of these changes.

As a Liberal Democrat councillor, I am committed to helping our community manage growth in a fair and sustainable way — listening to residents, finding practical solutions, and working constructively to get things done. Team work is essential in this endeavour.

I would be honoured to continue serving the diverse Trumpington community and working with you and my team for sustainable change. Please lend me and my teammate John Grimwood your

I’ve been living in Trumpington for the past thirteen years and it really feels like home now. These days, I share my house with my supportive wife, two kids and two lively dogs, while my two older step-children have flown the nest and started their own adventures.

I’ve worked in the NHS for almost twenty-five years. I used to be a front-line paramedic, but now my role has shifted behind the scenes. Even though I’m not out in the ambulance anymore, I still get to make a difference and support others in meaningful ways.

Alongside my NHS work, I also run a small business where I teach first aid and other courses. I love being able to share what I’ve learned and help others pick up important, life-saving skills. Juggling both my NHS role and my business keeps me active, but I find it incredibly rewarding and it lets me meet all sorts of people.

In addition to my work and business, I also volunteer with St John Ambulance, which allows me to further contribute to the wellbeing of my community and support people.

Whether it’s through my job, my business, or volunteering, I’m passionate about making a positive difference and supporting my community however I can.

I am delighted to be standing alongside Olaf Hauk as your candidates for City Council this May.

Contact John - john.grimwood.libdem@outlook.com

Raised between cultures, rooted in community, I understand what it means to build a life somewhere new, and I'm ready to bring that to Trumpington.

Like many in Trumpington, I was not born here, yet feel the warmth and hope of the people. I grew up on a London council estate as the child of working‑class Polish and Kurdish immigrant parents, learning early the importance of fairness and aspiration. My first experience in public service was as an elected youth councillor, where I successfully campaigned for frequent, and reliable, public transport.

Trumpington, at the heart of Cambridge’s technology corridor, deserves leaders who understand the importance of protecting public spaces, supporting social mobility, and standing firm against pressures from big investors. I work as a software engineer with experience across finance and some of Europe’s leading tech startups, where I’ve championed fair representation and supported unionisation. That experience has shown me first‑hand how large companies can shape,  and sometimes overshadow, our cities, and why Trumpington needs leadership that values innovation but never compromises accountability.

As your Green representative, I’ll prioritise sustainable development that puts people and the planet first. I see Trumpington’s newness as a strength: its cycling culture, wetlands, and community‑centred design already make it a model for green living. Together, we can build on that promise by tackling local issues like affordable housing, potholes, and earning Trumpington its first ofsted Outstanding rated school for its families, while protecting our natural spaces and ensuring the ward’s identity is set by its people.

I believe we need more Green voices to advocate for a reliable and affordable public transport network, genuinely affordable housing, more rights for tenants, and to respond adequately to the climate crisis. Greens have the policies to challenge the other parties on their poor response to the social and environmental challenges we’re facing in Cambridge. Now more than ever, I want to join our existing elected Greens in building a positive and sustainable future for Cambridge.

I am Carlos Toranzos. Cambridge has been my home for just over 40 years. I have lived in different wards, raised a family and sent my children to school and university here. Over the years I have experienced the joys and difficulties of city life. I have made friends, and have recognised that the people of Cambridge are generous, good-humoured, protective of their environment and caring of others.

This is how I introduced myself in the 2023 May elections. I was relatively new to these fields, I had been a trade union member and a representative of my university in areas of negotiations, etc., but not dealing with people the way I had to when canvassing in Trumpington. We worked hard and got a very good result in the elections – we were short of just 179 votes to win.

This experience made me realise that only direct contact with people can make you understand what is it that our society needs, and what people’s needs and aspirations are. This experience also made me realise that the only way things can be achieved is with commitment and hard work. Because of this, I have decided to stand again as Trumpington’s candidate for the May elections in 2025.

This decision was more the result of my contact with people in Trumpington. They made me realise that as a candidate you have to show commitment and desire to do your best to help everybody to be happy and engaged in our ward. Our youngsters showed me that they need a social club that encourages them to develop extracurricular activities. Our cyclists feel safe because the cycle paths are well looked after. Our pensioners made me realise that street lighting is essential for them to feel free to move around in winter. Our pedestrians showed me that the footpaths have to be well looked after, and that weeds like nettles must be pruned regularly to stop stinging them and their children even if they are in pushchairs or prams. With all these needs, I cannot be content with Trumpington councillors that do not take serious

I am a local resident in West Chesterton and have lived here my whole life. I am very proud of my home town and place of birth  and the leisure and green spaces that it provides. As an advocate for healthy living, I would love to see greater access to sports and leisure facilities across the city. As an avid cyclist, I understand that for many people in Cambridge including myself cycling is an integral part to our city wide identity.

For a number of years, during the summer months I have taken international students across the city on historic tours of the city. Recently, I have seen deterioration of public facilities within the city, including the closure of public toilets and inadequate maintenance of those that remain. I believe the city of Cambridge deserves better.

As a physiotherapy trainee and during my time at Addenbrookes I have an awareness of the importance of safeguarding our physical health. I believe the council has a responsibility to safeguard residents and to provide  them with sporting and leisure facilities,to aid wellbeing. I have seen a large increase in electric scooter related injuries recently and believe our city council isn’t doing enough to stop dangerous high-speed electric scooters on our commons and pavements.

As a psychology graduate, I am also passionate about mental health and believe the council should do more to foster a happy city by improving green areas and should end the local tennis court use fee that labour has introduced. I don’t believe taxation on physical activity is the correct way to support residents.

Contact Guy - mills.libdem@gmail.com

Having previous experience as a City Councillor, I have a track record of making tangible improvements to play areas, parks and green spaces and pushing the Labour-run council to improve its ambition, especially on climate change and biodiversity.

In my professional life, I am a hospital doctor and researcher, as well as a Trade Union representative defending NHS staff from unfair conditions. Since moving to West Chesterton, I have helped make sure pavements are safe and clear for pedestrians; worked to remove bollards which are obstructive to prams and wheelchairs. When previously a councillor, I spoke up for residents on many occasions at council, holding the Labour-run administration to account across key issues for our city; whether it be water, housing, the wider environment or ecology. I’m proud of my work as a community campaigner and hope that you will vote for more hard work in May.

● What am I about?

Issue based politics. This means my policies depend on the issue. I will use critical thinking, research and consultation to determine policy. I am not on the political spectrum. Not left, centre or right.

Protecting Arbury Court playpark (true also for the Liberals, Labour and Greens. Sorry, this should not help you decide. The Conservatives and Reform were not at the hustings so I do not know their stances).

Building a community centre (location to be determined).

Increasing the amount of green space (very obviously necessary having litterpicked and leafleted every reachable household).

Supporting building affordable homes. However, we must ALSO address all the abandoned and empty ones.

The nitty-gritty: public toilets, litter, bins, recycling, flytipping (prosecution needed), improving residents' parking schemes, potholes (the last two are county council responsibilities but I can advocate on them).

Action. There is too much party-politics. Let's try for consensus if possible and make improvements.

Eco-minded but not one-sided.

Meeting voters, listening properly then acting on it.

No doorknocking canvassing. It is your home. People have come out to talk to me.

I firmly believe the University of Cambridge and some huge corporations in Cambridge should morally do more for the city of Cambridge. They could easily sort out potholes, cracked pavements, add planters etc.

This does not absolve councils' responsibilities. Almost no one wants to pay more council tax (I have met some who want to. I listen).

To be 100% clear I am not asking small, medium or even large companies to contribute more. I have contacted ARM and AstraZeneca but no replies. They do have CSR budgets....

I will fight for all businesses in West Chesterton. I have tried to talk to you all. Thank you for your views on Business rates. In simple terms, the more successful you are the more you can employ.

I understand how hard it is for many young people in my ward. Gettin
